title: Add cache telemetry notes + flags to runtime release notes
preview:
## Summary
This PR updates the runtime release notes to document the new prefix/KV cache telemetry landing in this train, including:
- What new metrics exist and how to interpret them at a high level
- The config/feature flags required to enable the new signals safely
- Known limitations / caveats for the first production rollout

This is intended to reduce confusion during staged enablement (staging -> canary -> prod) and to make it clear which dashboards/alerts are expected to move when these flags are flipped.

## Context
Cache telemetry work has been landing across multiple PRs (counters, histograms, miss reasons, evictions/pressure, wasted prefill, sampling/guards, trace annotations, naming reconciliation). We’ve had multiple internal questions during canary setup about:
- whether hit-rate is expected to move immediately (and how sampling affects it)
- what labels should/shouldn’t be present (cardinality allowlists)
- what counts as a miss (e.g., version mismatch vs policy disabled)

This PR centralizes those notes in release notes so operators don’t have to piece it together from Slack threads.

## What’s included in the release notes
### New cache telemetry (high-level)
- KV cache hit/miss counters and derived hit-rate
- Prefix cache reuse histograms (reused token depth / reused tokens)
- Miss reason breakdown (capacity, ttl_expired, policy_disabled, hash_mismatch, version_mismatch)
- Eviction counts + eviction reason taxonomy, plus memory pressure indicators
- Wasted prefill (prefill tokens that did not contribute to subsequent decode due to misses/evictions)
- Trace span annotations for cache events (to correlate cache behavior with request latency without high-cardinality metrics)

### Flags / knobs (documented with defaults + rollout guidance)
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.enabled` (default: false in prod at time of this release; true in staging)
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.metrics_sampling_rate` (default: 0.05 in prod when enabled; 1.0 in staging)
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.event_rate_limit_per_sec` (protects against bursts on high-QPS routes)
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.labels_allowlist` (explicitly calls out that per-tenant / per-request labels are not emitted)
- `runtime.cache_telemetry.emit_trace_annotations` (default: true when tracing enabled; independent sampling)

### Known limitations / caveats (first rollout)
- Sampling: hit-rate should be stable in expectation, but low-volume routes will look noisy; guidance included for minimum traffic thresholds before acting on changes.
- Cardinality controls: some dimensions are intentionally bucketed/normalized (prompt-length buckets / token bins) and some are dropped at ingestion if misconfigured; notes include symptoms (e.g., “zero hits” due to label-drop) and where to look.
- Model/version rollouts: version mismatch misses can spike during canaries or when mixing versions across pools; recommendation is to verify routing/pinning policy when investigating sudden cost increases.
- Streaming retries: release notes point to the semantic change that retries should not double-count hits/misses once the retry-fix is present in this train.
- Continuous batching: notes clarify that hit attribution is per-request (not per-batch) and that extremely short prompts can show counter-intuitive reuse distributions.

### Pointers to operational docs
- Dashboards: Cache hit-rate + eviction pressure + wasted prefill
- Runbook: “Cache telemetry pipeline incident runbook” (what to do if metrics stop flowing / show zeros)

## Files changed
- `docs/release-notes/runtime/2025-03-xx.md`
  - Adds a “Cache telemetry (prefix/KV)” section with the above details
- `docs/ops/feature-flags.md`
  - Adds cache telemetry flags and short descriptions
- `docs/observability/metric-names.md`
  - Adds links to the canonical cache metric names and label policy

## Example snippet (as added)
Added to the runtime release note section:
"Cache telemetry is behind a feature flag. When enabled, expect new `cache.*` metrics to appear within ~5 minutes. Hit-rate and reuse histograms are sampled by default in production to limit overhead and cardinality. Investigate sudden hit-rate drops by first checking miss reason breakdown (version_mismatch, policy_disabled, capacity) and eviction pressure before adjusting cache knobs."

## Testing
- Doc-only changes.
- Verified links resolve in internal docs preview.

## Rollout notes
- Release notes explicitly state: do not flip `runtime.cache_telemetry.enabled` globally without confirming ingestion-side cardinality guards are deployed for the target region.

## Related
- ENG-4821 (definitions/tags)
- ENG-4824 (dashboards/alerts)
- PRs: #18421, #18422, #18423, #18424, #18425, #18426, #18428, #18430, #18431, #18432, #18433, #18434

---

## Conversation highlights
Allison Grant (review): Please add a short “If you see zero hits” troubleshooting note and link to the pipeline incident runbook. We’ve already had one canary where ingestion dropped labels and the dashboard looked flat.

Connor O'Brien (author): Added a troubleshooting callout under limitations + linked the runbook.

Logan Wright (review): Can we be explicit that per-tenant labels are not emitted (and will be dropped if someone tries to add them)? Also call out sampling making low-volume routes noisy.

Connor O'Brien (author): Done — added a “Cardinality + sampling” subsection with concrete symptoms.

Omar El-Sayed (review): Mention version_mismatch misses during rollouts. We saw that in SUP-3912 style investigations and it’s an easy misread as ‘cache broken’.

Connor O'Brien (author): Added a bullet under limitations plus guidance to check routing/pinning.

Sean Gallagher (review): Please avoid telling oncall to page on hit-rate changes; position as guardrail and link to alert policy.

Connor O'Brien (author): Updated wording to “guardrail” and removed paging language; added link to the SRE dashboard/alert policy doc.

Paula Mendes (PM): Looks good. This is sufficient for the MVP rollout; we can refine copy once Console surfaces the recommendations.

## CI
- Buildkite / `docs-lint`: PASS
- Buildkite / `unit`: PASS (no-op)
- Link checker: PASS

## Merge
Squash merged into `main`.

title: Add cache telemetry overhead perf-canary gate (p95 regression threshold)
preview:
Adds a new perf-canary workload and CI gate to quantify runtime overhead from the prefix/KV cache telemetry instrumentation. The canary runs a stable request mix with caching enabled and toggles telemetry flags to compare p95 end-to-end latency (prefill+decode) against a baseline. If the measured p95 regression exceeds the configured threshold, the canary fails to prevent rollouts that silently add latency overhead.

Motivation: We’re rolling out new cache metrics (hit/miss counters, eviction pressure, wasted prefill) behind flags and with sampling, but we need a hard stop in CI to keep instrumentation overhead near-zero (especially at p95/p99). This PR is intended to be the guardrail referenced by the runtime rollout plan and SRE approval thread.

What’s included: (1) New scenario `cache_telemetry_overhead` that replays a prompt set designed to exercise prefill, decode, prefix reuse, and steady-state KV reuse; includes prompt-length buckets (short/med/long) and a chat-like multi-turn loop to catch streaming-related paths. (2) A/B run harness: A = telemetry disabled, B = telemetry enabled at production defaults (sampling and rate limits on). Each arm is run N times and we take the median p95 to reduce flake. (3) Regression gate: fails if (B_p95 - A_p95) / A_p95 > 0.015 (1.5%) with a minimum absolute delta floor of 0.7ms to avoid noise-triggered failures on very fast hardware. (4) Artifacts: uploads JSON summary including p50/p95/p99, CPU util, and “telemetry event rate” counters emitted by the harness (not runtime metrics) so we can debug abnormal deltas. (5) Documentation: adds runbook-ish notes to the perf-canary README and exposes a single knob for temporarily widening the threshold via repo config (requires CODEOWNERS approval).

Notes/assumptions: This canary measures end-to-end request latency at the client boundary; it does not attempt to attribute time spent in metrics emission vs request processing. The goal is gating and early detection, not perfect profiling. The scenario uses deterministic seeds and fixed concurrency to minimize scheduler variance; it is pinned to a single GPU type in CI.

Rollout plan: Land in perf-canary first, run for 24h on main with threshold in “report-only” mode (configured via CI env var). Then flip to enforced mode once we confirm stability. This matches the broader cache telemetry rollout gate requirements (staging -> canary -> prod).

title: Alerts: experiment config propagation lag + attribution anomalies (route experiments)
preview: Context: Route-level experiments rely on control-plane config propagation into the routing/policy layer. During beta we observed occasional propagation lag and a few classes of attribution anomalies (missing experiment_id/variant_id tags, variant receiving ~0 traffic due to config/eligibility issues, and split skew early in rollout). This PR adds Prometheus/Alertmanager rules and annotations for the standard route experiments dashboards, with SRE-reviewed runbook links and recommended severities. Scope: (1) propagation lag/staleness alerts tied to the new propagation lag metric emitted by the redwood monorepo, (2) attribution drop-rate and missing-dimension alerts for experiment metrics/traces aggregation, (3) “variant 0 traffic” detection to catch misconfigured allocations or eligibility rules. Notes: rules are written to avoid cardinality blow-ups (no per-route fanout by default); high-cardinality drilldowns remain in Grafana dashboards. Related: redwood PR-28631 (adds config propagation lag metric), redwood PR-28734 (runbook URLs in alert payloads), observability-pack PR-1934 (dashboards). Threads: slack/eng-sre/1736455521-alert-thresholds-experiment-propagation-lag.json and slack/eng-sre/1738362044-attribution-drop-rate-alert-noise.json informed thresholds/windows. Rollout: enable in staging immediately; prod enabled behind the existing observability-pack release gate (see release notes section).

title: Fix missing experiment attribution on streaming retries
preview: Context: During the route experiments beta we saw a subset of streaming requests (SSE) lose experiment attribution after a retry, which caused (a) undercounted per-variant traffic, (b) misleading guardrail charts, and (c) confusing traces where the initial attempt was tagged but the retry attempt was not. This was escalated via SUP-24877 and corroborated by staging attribution-drop dashboards. Root cause: Our streaming retry path reconstructs a new RequestContext / SpanContext for the retry attempt. The base routing decision (including experiment assignment) was persisted on the first attempt, but the retry code path re-evaluated portions of the request pipeline and did not copy experiment dimensions into the retry context. In some cases we also emitted the streaming response headers from the retry attempt before experiment tags were re-attached, so x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-experiment-variant were missing even though the route was in an active experiment. Changes: (1) Persist experiment assignment (experiment_id, variant_id, config_version) into the per-request RetryState and rehydrate it into the retry RequestContext before any OTel spans/metrics are emitted. (2) Ensure the streaming response header writer reads attribution from RequestContext (not transient span tags) so retries remain consistent. (3) Add a guard to prevent re-bucketing on retry unless the original assignment is absent (maintains determinism and prevents variant flipping mid-stream). (4) Add coverage: integration test exercising streaming + injected mid-stream network failure + retry, asserting that experiment dimensions remain stable across attempts and are present on emitted spans/metrics and response debug headers. Safety/rollout: This is behind the existing route experiments feature flag; behavior only changes when retries occur. Added a metric counter for attribution_rehydrated_on_retry to validate impact in canary. Linked work: ADR-048 (attribution schema), PR-28470 (request context plumbing).
text: Context: During the route experiments beta we saw a subset of streaming requests (SSE) lose experiment attribution after a retry, which caused (a) undercounted per-variant traffic, (b) misleading guardrail charts, and (c) confusing traces where the initial attempt was tagged but the retry attempt was not. This was escalated via SUP-24877 and corroborated by staging attribution-drop dashboards. Root cause: Our streaming retry path reconstructs a new RequestContext / SpanContext for the retry attempt. The base routing decision (including experiment assignment) was persisted on the first attempt, but the retry code path re-evaluated portions of the request pipeline and did not copy experiment dimensions into the retry context. In some cases we also emitted the streaming response headers from the retry attempt before experiment tags were re-attached, so x-redwood-experiment-id / x-redwood-experiment-variant were missing even though the route was in an active experiment. title: Add retention verification + compliance pack generation dashboards/alerts
preview:
Summary: This PR adds the first version of the retention/compliance dashboards and alerting rules needed for the “Retention controls + compliance reporting pack” program. It introduces (1) Retention Enforcement Verification dashboards (TTL lag, purge throughput/failures, backlog) and (2) Compliance Report Pack Generation SLIs (success rate + latency) with corresponding alerts. It also updates the repo README to document metric prerequisites and rollout guidance.

Context / why: We’ve had multiple incidents/tickets where enforcement was “working” but not observable at the right level (e.g., policy applied but TTL lag growing in a single region; purge jobs silently throttling after maintenance). Security/GRC also needs a customer-ready way to evidence enforcement (at least internally) and to demonstrate the compliance pack generator’s reliability as we start using it for enterprise security reviews.

What’s included:
- Dashboards:
  - “Retention Controls: Verification (Overview)”
    - TTL lag (p50/p90/p99) by store + region
    - Purge throughput (rows deleted / min) and runtime
    - Purge failures (rate + top error reasons when exported)
    - Backlog / lag trend line for the last 7d
  - “Retention Controls: Purge Jobs (Deep Dive)”
    - Per-job panels (request-logs, audit-logs, traces, metrics, artifacts where applicable)
    - Retry volume + idempotent re-run indicators (based on job outcome labels)
  - “Compliance Pack: Generation SLI/SLO”
    - SLI: report_pack_generation_success_rate (derived)
    - Latency: report_pack_generation_duration_seconds (p50/p90/p99)
    - Queue/backlog (if enabled) and saturation signals
- Alerts (Prometheus rules):
  - RetentionTTLTooHigh (warning/page)
    - Warning: ttl_lag_seconds{store=~"request_logs|audit_logs"} p99 > 6h for 30m
    - Page: p99 > 24h for 15m (per region) OR backlog increasing for 60m
  - RetentionPurgeFailuresHigh (page)
    - purge_failures_total increase > 10 in 15m per job_type/region
  - RetentionPurgeNoProgress (warning)
    - purge_rows_deleted_total no increase for 2h while ttl_lag_seconds p90 > 2h
  - CompliancePackGenerationSLOViolation (warning/page)
    - Warning: success rate < 99.5% over 1h
    - Page: success rate < 98% over 30m OR p90 duration > 15m over 1h
- README updates:
  - Documents required metrics + labels (store, region, job_type, tenant_tier)
  - Notes on cardinality: alerts intentionally avoid tenant_id; dashboards allow optional filtering but default views are store/region only
  - Rollout instructions: enable dashboards first, then alerts at warning-only for 1 week before paging

Metric dependencies (expected to exist from the product/runtime PRs):
- ttl_lag_seconds{store,region}
- purge_rows_deleted_total{store,region,job_type}
- purge_failures_total{store,region,job_type,reason?}
- purge_job_duration_seconds{store,region,job_type}
- report_pack_generation_duration_seconds{region,outcome}
- report_pack_generation_total{region,outcome}

Implementation notes:
- Alert thresholds were chosen to match SRE guidance in the retention purge runbook: lag > 24h is a customer-impacting condition (policy drift / failure to meet retention), while shorter lags are warning signals that typically correlate with DB throttling or a stuck worker.
- We added a small “debug” row in the deep-dive dashboard for correlating lag spikes to purge job runtime, failures, and DB maintenance windows (annotation placeholder; actual annotation source is separate).
- Dashboards follow the existing Jsonnet conventions in this repo; panel UIDs are stable and include ‘retention-*’ prefixes for easy lookup.

Testing:
- Rendered dashboards locally (make dashboards) and validated against a staging Prometheus snapshot.
- promtool rule checks pass.
- Verified that rule expressions don’t use tenant_id and don’t blow up cardinality with region x store x job_type only.

Rollout plan:
- Ship dashboards immediately (safe).
- Enable alerts in warning-only in staging for 1 week; then promote to production warning-only for 1 week; then turn on paging routes.

References:
- Linear: ENG-18452 (verification dashboards/alerts), PM-842 (initiative), ENG-18441 (report pack generator)
- Related product metrics PRs: redwood/pr-28540-add-retention-verification-metrics, audit-log-exporter/pr-612-add-time-windowed-exports-and-filters

Screenshots: Added to the README section “Retention & Compliance Dashboards” (rendered preview images) and attached in the PR for review.

title: Dedicated throughput SLO: add dashboard panels for floor tracking, saturation, and drilldowns
preview:
Context: We’re rolling out a formal Dedicated throughput SLO and need a consistent, low-friction dashboard experience for SRE/CS and (later) a curated subset for customers. This PR extends the dedicated mixin dashboards with panels that (a) track throughput floors over time per customer/deployment, (b) show saturation signals that correlate with SLO misses, and (c) provide drilldowns into the exact deployment/model/region slice.

What’s included: 1) “Throughput floor vs actual” panels: tokens/sec (per deployment_id) overlaid with computed floor (tier + shape) and SLO evaluator compliance; includes 5m/30m rollups with clear “below floor” highlighting. 2) “Saturation signals” row: admission-control shed rate, queue depth, in-flight requests, batcher wait time, KV cache hit ratio (when available), and GPU utilization proxy (SM occupancy / active time from node exporter where present). 3) Drilldowns: links from customer-level panels → deployment-level panels, and deployment-level → explore queries + logs/traces (requires `deployment_id` label) with pre-filled time range and filters. 4) Variables & filtering: standardized template variables (`customer_id`, `deployment_id`, `tier`, `region`, `model_family`) and a default view that emphasizes Dedicated pools only. 5) Runbook hooks: adds inline links to the Dedicated throughput SLO escalation runbook and the tier-aware routing/overload protection runbook.

Query semantics: Uses the canonical per-deployment throughput metric emitted by runtime (`redwood_inference_tokens_total` / `rate`), with streaming-aware token accounting (relies on the runtime fix merged separately; this PR avoids older `stream_tokens_total` series). Floor overlay is sourced from the SLO evaluator summary series (`dedicated_throughput_floor_tokens_per_sec`) when available; falls back to a static tier mapping panel note when the evaluator series is missing in a region.

Screenshots: Not attached; dashboards can be rendered locally via `make dashboards` and compared in Grafana using the “mixin-preview” folder output.

Rollout: Safe to merge; panels are behind the existing Dedicated dashboard folder and only appear where the required metrics exist. Follow-up: once the customer-facing Console widget lands, we’ll likely want a simplified “status” view fed by the same evaluator series.

title: Dedicated throughput SLO: add burn-rate alerts + wire to paging policies
preview: Context: As part of Dedicated throughput guarantees, we need first-class SLO alerting that is (a) burn-rate based (fast/slow), (b) scoped per Dedicated deployment, and (c) correctly routed to oncall + CS escalation paths. This PR adds burn-rate alert rules for the Dedicated Throughput Floor SLO and wires them into our paging policies. It also adds the runbook links and dashboard deep-links to make the alerts actionable. Scope: - New Prometheus rules for Dedicated throughput SLO burn-rate (page + ticket) - Tier-aware routing labels (Standard/Plus/Premium) to support different urgency/paging - Alertmanager routes + PagerDuty service mapping (SRE oncall; non-page tickets to #customer-success triage) - Annotations: customer/deployment identifiers, region, model, and links to the Dedicated Throughput dashboard panels - Guardrails: suppress alerts when deployment is in provisioning/warmup window or explicitly marked 'customer_throttled' by admission-control telemetry Notes: The burn-rate computations follow the SLO Toolkit conventions (fast: 2h window, slow: 24h window) but are implemented using Redwood’s in-repo rule templates to avoid an additional dependency at runtime. Related: ENG-19244 (metric emission), ENG-19257 (tier-aware admission control).

title: Telemetry: cardinality guardrails for unit economics tags
preview:
Summary
- Adds hard validation + enforcement guardrails for high-cardinality tag dimensions used by the Unit Economics pipeline (model_id/model_version, region, customer_tier, quantization_profile, route_group, streaming).
- Prevents accidental explosions in metrics cardinality (e.g. route_id, raw customer identifiers, arbitrary quantization profile strings) that have previously caused ingestion lag and warehouse view instability.
- Emits explicit counters for dropped/rewritten tags so we can alert before dashboard coverage degrades.

Context
The internal Unit Economics dashboard depends on stable metering dimensions (see Confluence: token-metering-dimensions-and-cardinality). Over the last few weeks we’ve seen several near-misses where a single deploy introduced unbounded values in a tag (route id with embedded customer UUID; quantization profile leaking experiment run ids), which then:
- increased metrics series count significantly,
- delayed downstream rollups, and
- made hourly aggregates partially missing.

This PR implements guardrails at the telemetry emission boundary for the economics-related meter so we fail “safe” (map to canonical buckets) rather than “fail noisy” (explode series / drop ingestion).

Design (what changed)
1) Central TagPolicy and enforcement library
- Introduces telemetry/tag_guardrails (shared lib used by gateway + runtime meter emitter).
- Policy applied per dimension key:
  - max_value_length (truncate + mark)
  - allowed_values allowlist (optional)
  - allowed_regex (optional)
  - max_unique_values_per_window (per-process unique tracker; enforces using LRU+TTL)
  - fallback_value (what we emit when invalid / over limit)

2) Canonicalization changes for known offenders
- route_id is no longer emitted as a metric tag for unit economics. We emit route_group instead.
  - route_group is derived from route metadata (stable, low cardinality) and defaults to "unknown".
  - For debugging, the full route_id is still available in traces/logs (NOT as a metrics tag).
- quantization_profile is validated against a fixed set of allowed values (from model metadata config) and normalized (e.g. "int4-awq" and "INT4_AWQ" -> "int4_awq"). Unknown values map to "unknown".

3) Visibility / safety knobs
- New metrics:
  - telemetry.tag_guardrails.rewritten_total{meter,dimension,reason}
  - telemetry.tag_guardrails.dropped_total{meter,dimension,reason}
  - telemetry.tag_guardrails.unique_limit_exceeded_total{meter,dimension}
- New feature flag:
  - telemetry.tag_guardrails.unit_econ_v1
    - off: no guardrails
    - shadow: validate + emit counters, but do not rewrite/drop
    - enforce: rewrite/drop per policy

4) Aggregation compatibility
- Warehouse rollups already treat "unknown" as a valid bucket. This PR standardizes the fallback values and updates the dimension dictionary in-code comments.

Rollout plan
- Land behind feature flag in shadow mode (24h) in prod.
- SRE/Observability to review rewritten/dropped counters and confirm no unexpected churn.
- Flip to enforce during a low-traffic window.

Testing
- Unit tests for TagPolicy (regex/allowlist/length/unique-window behavior).
- Integration-style tests for the unit-econ meter emitter (ensures we never emit disallowed dimensions; ensures stable fallback values).

Notes / follow-ups
- Follow-up PR will wire guardrail counters into the unit econ pipeline Grafana pack and add an alert when rewritten_total exceeds threshold for >15m (tracked in ENG-8429).

Risk assessment
- Low product risk: only affects internal unit-econ metering tags.
- Moderate observability risk if we incorrectly bucket a tag; mitigated via shadow-mode + metrics.

Screenshots
- N/A (backend only)

Changelog
- Internal-only telemetry behavior change; no external API impact.

title: Add Private backup/restore dashboards + alert rules (backup success, duration, restore drill outcomes)
preview: Context: As part of the Private Day-2 backup/restore toolkit, we need an opinionated observability pack that SRE/Solutions can deploy into customer VPC/on-prem environments to answer two questions quickly: (1) are backups happening and completing successfully on schedule, and (2) are restore drills passing and meeting RTO targets in staging. This PR adds a set of dashboards and alert rules designed to work across hosted VPC installs and constrained on-prem/air-gapped setups (where available metrics are limited and log shipping may be disabled). Scope: (A) dashboards for backup job success rate, last_success_ts, duration, bytes, and error categories; (B) dashboards for restore drill outcomes (pass/fail, duration, validation phase breakdown) and drill cadence; (C) alert rules with conservative defaults plus documented tuning for noisy environments (slow object storage, intermittent KMS throttling, planned maintenance). Metrics assumed: redwood_private_backup_job_success_total, redwood_private_backup_job_duration_seconds, redwood_private_backup_bytes_total, redwood_private_backup_last_success_timestamp_seconds, redwood_private_restore_drill_success_total, redwood_private_restore_drill_duration_seconds, redwood_private_restore_validation_phase_duration_seconds, and redwood_private_backup_error_total{category=...} (emitted by control-plane + installer hooks in related PRs). Dashboards/alerts are grouped under a new folder 'Redwood Private / Backup & DR'. Tuning notes: alert thresholds use per-install labels (deployment_id, environment, cluster) and are designed to avoid paging during planned maintenance by supporting a maintenance label/recording rule override; duration alerts are based on p95 over a 6h window to reduce sensitivity to occasional NFS/S3 jitter; stale backup alert uses last_success_ts with configurable expected_interval (default 24h) and grace window (default +6h). Out of scope: defining the core metrics emission (handled in redwood monorepo), paging policy selection (SRE runbook), and customer-specific routing/notification integrations. Validation: Tested in staging with a simulated failing backup job and an intentionally broken restore drill; ensured alerts fire and resolve; verified dashboard variables work when some metric series are absent (air-gapped mode without bytes_total). Screenshots are attached in the PR comments (not committed).

title: Dedicated: emit burst-capacity metering events (committed vs burst) for billing exports
preview: Context: Dedicated tiers require billing/usage export to separate committed throughput consumption from burst capacity so Finance/Sales/CS can explain invoices and enforce tier policies (ENG-19302). This PR introduces a canonical metering event for burst usage, wires it into the request accounting path, and adds a daily aggregation job that produces committed_vs_burst totals per deployment_id/model/region. Definitions: (1) committed_usage_tokens = tokens attributed while the deployment is within its committed throughput floor capacity slice, (2) burst_usage_tokens = tokens attributed while the deployment consumes burst pool capacity above its committed slice, (3) unmetered_tokens = tokens excluded from billing exports (e.g., internal health checks, warmup). Attribution is computed at request finalization using the tier-aware admission/routing decision already attached to the request context (pool_assignment + capacity_class). Guardrails: feature flag `metering.dedicated_burst_v1` default off; if the capacity_class tag is missing, we fall back to committed (to avoid under-billing) and emit a counter `metering_burst_missing_capacity_class_total` for cleanup. Also includes unit tests for streaming vs non-streaming to ensure we do not double-count tokens, and adds a small schema version bump for the billing export (new columns) behind `export.schema_version >= 2025_02_25`. Rollout plan: enable flag for 2 internal Dedicated deployments, validate dashboards/export parity for 72h, then expand to beta customers selected by CS; Finance to validate sample invoices before GA.

title: Routing: map Dedicated tier → pool/shape selector (feature-flagged, safe defaults)
preview:
## Summary
This PR adds a tier-aware pool/shape selector to the router so Dedicated requests can be mapped deterministically to a hardware pool (and optionally a shape) based on customer tier (Standard/Plus/Premium) and region availability. The change is gated behind a feature flag and defaults to current routing behavior when tier metadata is missing or the selector cannot find a compatible target.

Key goals:
- Provide a single canonical mapping tier → pool/shape used by the control plane + router.
- Make behavior safe-by-default (no tier info or config mismatch should not break routing).
- Enable gradual rollout via feature flag + shadow evaluation metrics.

Related: ENG-19263 (Tier-aware routing policy tier→pool mapping).

## Motivation / context
Dedicated throughput SLOs require consistent capacity accounting and predictable placement. Today, Dedicated deployments may route to whatever compatible pool has capacity, which makes it harder to enforce per-tier isolation/headroom and creates ambiguous failure modes when fleet is constrained.

This PR introduces a config-driven selector that:
- Reads tier and deployment constraints (region, model, context limits, required capabilities).
- Selects a preferred pool/shape target for that tier.
- Allows fallbacks in a controlled order (same tier in-region → same tier cross-region if allowed → legacy selection if flag disabled).

## Design
### Inputs
- deployment_id (Dedicated)
- customer_tier (enum: standard/plus/premium; absent treated as unknown)
- region (request region)
- model_id + model_version (required for compatibility checks)
- request capabilities (streaming/tool calling) used for pool capability filtering
- policy config (tier mapping, fallback rules)

### Outputs
- pool_id (required)
- optional shape_id (when the pool is subdivided into shapes)
- decision metadata (reason codes) for metrics/debugging

### Safe defaults
- If feature flag is OFF: route with existing selection logic.
- If tier is missing/unknown: route with existing selection logic (and emit metric: tier_selector.unknown_tier).
- If config is present but no eligible pool is found: fall back to existing selection logic (metric: tier_selector.no_match).

### Feature flags
- `routing.tier_pool_selector.enabled` (bool): enables enforcement.
- `routing.tier_pool_selector.shadow_eval` (bool): runs selector and emits decision metrics but does not enforce.

In the initial rollout we plan to run shadow_eval for ~1 week, validate match rates, then enable enforcement for internal/beta Dedicated accounts.

## Implementation notes
- Adds `TierAwarePoolSelector` in routing/orchestration.
- Adds config schema + validation for tier mapping and fallback policy.
- Extends request context to carry `customer_tier` for Dedicated deployments (wired from control plane metadata; falls back to empty).
- Adds metrics:
  - `router.tier_selector.decision_total{tier,region,result,reason}`
  - `router.tier_selector.shadow_mismatch_total{tier,region}` (shadow decision differs from legacy selector)
  - `router.tier_selector.fallback_total{tier,region,fallback_level}`
- Adds structured logs with decision metadata (deployment_id, tier, selected_pool, selected_shape, reasons).

## Testing
- Unit tests cover:
  - Tier mapping happy paths
  - Unknown tier/missing tier
  - No-match fallback
  - Capability/model compatibility filtering
  - Cross-region fallback blocked/allowed
- Integration test (router sim) verifies:
  - Shadow mode emits decisions without affecting routing
  - Enabled mode pins the selected pool

## Rollout plan
1) Land behind flags (this PR).
2) Enable `shadow_eval` in staging + a small internal Dedicated deployment.
3) Enable enforcement for beta accounts (feature-flag allowlist).
4) Expand by region as fleet shape catalog stabilizes.

## Follow-ups (out of scope)
- Wire in explicit shape recommendations from the shape catalog service (today shape_id is only used when config pins one).
- Add admin tooling to preview tier → pool mapping per region for Sales/CS.
- Connect selector reason codes to oncall runbook links (SRE request).

## Commits
- routing: add TierAwarePoolSelector + decision reason codes
- control-plane: plumb Dedicated tier onto routing context
- config: add tier-to-pool mapping schema + validation
- metrics/logging: emit tier selector decisions + shadow mismatch
- tests: unit + router sim integration tests

## Changed areas
- routing/orchestrator/
- control_plane/deployments/
- config/policies/
- metrics/
- tests/router_sim/

## Review discussion (highlights)
- Mina Haddad: Requested clearer precedence rules between explicit customer pool pinning vs tier mapping. Updated selector to honor explicit deployment pool pin first, then tier mapping, then legacy.
- Ravi Menon: Asked to keep the legacy selector as a pure fallback to avoid subtle behavior changes. Added explicit flag gates and reason-coded fallback metrics.
- Irene Choi / Vanessa Ortiz: Asked for guardrails around cross-region fallback since it affects capacity accounting. Defaulted cross-region fallback to OFF unless explicitly configured per tier.
- Sean Gallagher: Asked for consistent semantics in metrics to support SLO dashboards. Added stable `result`/`reason` label sets and a shadow mismatch counter to estimate behavior delta before enforcement.

## CI
- Build: pass
- Unit tests: pass
- Lint: pass
- Router sim integration: pass

## Merge
Merged via squash to main. Feature remains disabled by default (flag off).

title: [LTS 1.18] Fix Sev1 crash in KV cache compaction (backport)
preview:
Context
This is a targeted backport to runtime-1.18-lts for a Sev1 crash in KV cache compaction reported during LTS pilot validation and via SUP-18544. The crash manifests as a use-after-free / invalid access during compaction when a block is concurrently scheduled for eviction under memory pressure. We are treating this as LTS-eligible (correctness + crash) and release-blocking for 1.18.1-lts RC.

Root cause
Compaction iterates over a segment list while eviction can detach/free segments after a memory-pressure callback. On main, this was mitigated by refcounted pinning and a stricter lock ordering (compaction acquires segment_lock before eviction_lock). In the 1.18 LTS branch, we still had the older code path where compaction relied on "best-effort" pointer stability and a non-atomic "in_compaction" flag, which is insufficient under bursty workloads with aggressive eviction.

Fix
- Introduce segment pinning during compaction: compaction now takes a strong ref to the segment header before walking blocks; eviction checks pin count and defers freeing until pins drop.
- Enforce lock ordering to avoid a rare deadlock we saw in a local stress test when trying the naive fix (documented in code comment).
- Harden guardrails: if compaction detects a segment state transition mid-walk (evict_pending), it bails out and retries rather than continuing with stale offsets.

Why safe for LTS
- Minimal diff relative to mainline fix; avoids bringing in unrelated KV cache changes that landed after 1.18 branch cut.
- No behavior change on the steady state path except an extra atomic inc/dec around segments being compacted.
- Added focused tests + ran perf gates to validate no measurable regression within LTS thresholds.

Testing
- Added unit test that reproduces the crash by interleaving compaction + eviction on a mocked clock and deterministic executor.
- Added GPU smoke test variant that forces compaction every N tokens with small KV budget to trigger frequent eviction.
- Ran scheduler regression suite (release branch) to ensure no cross-component fallout.

Perf validation notes (perf-canary)
Benchmarks ran on A100-80G and H100, workloads: chat-mixed (short+long), summarization-long, and embeddings-batch. Results vs runtime-1.18.0-lts baseline: 
- Throughput: within -0.3% to +0.2% (noise)
- p95 latency: +0.4% worst-case on summarization-long (within gate)
- Memory: no increase in steady-state KV footprint; transient pinning increases peak pinned segments by <= 2 in stress mode only

Rollout / release notes
- This should be called out under "Stability" in 1.18.1-lts release notes: "Fixes a rare but high-severity crash in KV cache compaction under eviction pressure."
- No config changes required.

References
- Jira: SUP-18544
- Related LTS epic: ENG-4821

Checklist
- [x] Backport eligibility confirmed (Sev1 crash)
- [x] Focused regression test added
- [x] GPU smoke test executed on release branch
- [x] Perf-canary run and within LTS thresholds
- [x] SRE notified for pilot rollout monitoring
- [x] Changelog note drafted for 1.18.1-lts

title: Add time-windowed exports + actor/event-type filters (report templates)
preview:
## Summary
This PR enhances `audit-log-exporter` to support compliance/evidence workflows by adding (1) explicit time-windowed exports and (2) optional filters for actor + event types. It also introduces a small report-template layer so the compliance reporting pack can request a known preset ("last_90_days_admin_activity", "rbac_changes", etc.) without re-encoding filter logic in multiple places.

Motivation: enterprise security reviews are consistently asking for "audit logs for a defined period" and "only admin/security-relevant events". We also had a support report (SUP-3917) where customers believed exports were missing event types; part of that was unclear defaults and lack of an explicit event-type filter list in the export request.

## API changes
### New request fields (non-breaking)
- `start_time` (RFC3339, inclusive)
- `end_time` (RFC3339, exclusive)
- `actor_ids` (optional array of strings)
- `actor_types` (optional array: `user|service_account|api_key`)
- `event_types` (optional array of strings; validated against known types)
- `template_id` (optional string; mutually exclusive with providing filters directly unless `allow_override=true`)

Behavior:
- If `start_time`/`end_time` are omitted, behavior remains "export all available within retention" (existing behavior).
- If provided, exporter enforces the window at query time and re-checks in stream/iteration to avoid boundary drift.
- `event_types` is an allowlist filter; unknown types return 400 with a list of valid types.
- `actor_ids` and `actor_types` are ANDed with the time-window and event filter.

### Report templates
Adds `templates/*.yaml` defining a stable set of presets that map to filters + default time windows. Example preset:
- `rbac_changes_last_180d` -> `event_types=[rbac.role.created, rbac.role.updated, rbac.binding.created, rbac.binding.deleted]`, window=180d

Template contract:
- Templates are versioned by exporter release; template IDs are stable.
- Export response includes `applied_filters` so customers/auditors can see exactly what was used.

## Implementation notes
- Query layer updated to accept a `FilterSpec` that compiles to SQL WHERE clauses + parameter binding.
- Streaming export code now emits a header block with: `export_id`, `generated_at`, `window`, `filters`, `template_id` (if any).
- Added a canonical "known event types" registry and tests ensuring registry coverage matches the producer schema we expect.
- Pagination: time-windowed exports use `(timestamp, id)` cursor to avoid duplicates when multiple events share the same timestamp.

## Security/Compliance considerations
- Filters are validated server-side (no raw SQL fragments; strict param binding).
- `actor_ids` is limited to 200 entries to cap query size.
- Maximum export window defaults to 365 days unless `--allow-large-windows` is enabled (intended for internal GRC workflows only).
- Added explicit audit event for `audit_log.export.generated` that records: caller identity, window, filters, and bytes written.

## Testing
- Unit tests: filter compilation, RFC3339 parsing, template resolution, unknown event-type validation.
- Integration tests: seeded audit events across boundary timestamps; verifies inclusive/exclusive semantics.
- Load sanity: ran export for 90d in staging with 1.2M rows; query time stable and memory constant in streaming mode.

## Rollout
- Default behavior unchanged. Consumers can adopt templates incrementally.
- Follow-up: wire `template_id` usage into compliance report pack generator (ENG-18441) once this merges.

## Example request
`POST /v1/exports`
{
  "template_id": "rbac_changes_last_180d",
  "start_time": "2024-08-01T00:00:00Z",
  "end_time": "2025-02-01T00:00:00Z"
}

## Checklist
- [x] Backwards compatible
- [x] Tests added
- [x] Docs updated (README + examples)
- [x] Security review notes captured (filters + limits)
- [x] Added exporter-side audit event for export generation
